coredata.dc:title: Ultra-small highly fluorescent zinc-based metal organic framework nanodots for ratiometric visual sensing of tetracycline based on aggregation induced emission
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammed Ameen, Sameera Sh
Sher Mohammed, Nidhal M.
Omer, Khalid M.
coredata.dc:description: Color tonality by intrinsic fluorescent metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) is highly desirable in bioanalytical applications due to its stability, low-cost and robustness with no need for functionalization and/or encapsulation of fluorophores. In the present work, ultra-small and higly fluorescent zinc-based MOFs (FMOF-5) were synthesized. The prepared FMOFs were around 5 nm in size, and gave strong blue emission at 440 nm when excited at 350 nm. Interestingly, tetracycline (TC) selectively tuned the blue emission of FMOF-5 to greenish-yellow emission (520 nm) with dramatic enhancement through aggregation induced emission (AIE). The fluorimetric analysis of TC was carried out through the ratiometric peak intensities of F520/F440, with detection limit (LOD) of 5 nM. To realize quantitative point-of-care based on color tonality, a smartphone integrated with the ratiometric visual platform was thereby design. Hence, TC was visually detected with LOD of 10 nM. The prepared FMOF-5-based probe showed high stability (3 months) and reusability (∼10 times). The developed visual-based platform presents great promise for practical point of care testing due to its low-cost, robustness, ruggedness, simple operation, and excellent selectivity and repeatability.

head.ce:title: Ultra-small highly fluorescent zinc-based metal organic framework nanodots for ratiometric visual sensing of tetracycline based on aggregation induced emission

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Color tonality by intrinsic fluorescent metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) is highly desirable in bioanalytical applications due to its stability, low-cost and robustness with no need for functionalization and/or encapsulation of fluorophores. In the present work, ultra-small and higly fluorescent zinc-based MOFs (FMOF-5) were synthesized. The prepared FMOFs were around 5 nm in size, and gave strong blue emission at 440 nm when excited at 350 nm. Interestingly, tetracycline (TC) selectively tuned the blue emission of FMOF-5 to greenish-yellow emission (520 nm) with dramatic enhancement through aggregation induced emission (AIE). The fluorimetric analysis of TC was carried out through the ratiometric peak intensities of F520/F440, with detection limit (LOD) of 5 nM. To realize quantitative point-of-care based on color tonality, a smartphone integrated with the ratiometric visual platform was thereby design. Hence, TC was visually detected with LOD of 10 nM. The prepared FMOF-5-based probe showed high stability (3 months) and reusability (∼10 times). The developed visual-based platform presents great promise for practical point of care testing due to its low-cost, robustness, ruggedness, simple operation, and excellent selectivity and repeatability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Developing effective visual-based sensing is very essential for clinical diagnosis and pharmaceutical analysis particularly in resource-limited settings [1,2]. Current trends in bioanalysis favor instrument-free detection, non-toxic precursors, precise and accurate results, and simplicity of use [3–5]. On-site detection is highly critical in bioanalysis especially for antibiotics analysis, due to the needs for the safety of human being, social economy as well as consumer perception and demand.

Tetracycline (TC) is widely used as a drug of infectious diseases, growth promoter, control bacterial infections of animal feed, and improve livestock production [6,7]. Seeing the extensive amounts of TC utilized worldwide, monitoring of this type of antibiotics is paramount. Therefore attention must be given in the analysis of TC to assess correct amount in food, feedstuffs, and residues in relevant environmental samples [6,8]. Thus, monitoring of TC levels requires a quick, reliable, selective, and sensitive detection method with low-cost, and safety analysis [9–12].

Various methods have been used for detection of TC, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [13,14], electrochemical [7], colorimetric [15], and capillary electrophoresis (CE) [16]. Although, some of these methods have acceptable sensitivity and selectivity, but they are costly, time-consuming and require sophisticated sample pretreatment and skilled personnel. Thus, they are undesirable for the real-time and on-site detection. To this end, attempt should be made to develop a method with fast response, instrument-free, and high accuracy and precision with on-site detection.

In recent years, fluorescence-based analysis has been applied for TC detection. Most of the developed probes were quenching-based mode (turn-off) or enhancement-based mode (turn-on) using a single fluorescence signal (i.e. intensity based mode) [17–21]. This intensity of a single emission is usually influenced by many environmental factors that resulting in errors. To address this problem, it is rational to build ratiometric fluorescence (dual-signal) either from one or two emitters to overcome the shortcomings of a single-emission fluorescence [22,23]. In ratiometric probes, the fluorescence intensity ratio at two well-resolved wavelengths offers a built-in correction against external interferences. Thus, to overcome false signals originating from environmental effects is essential to improve signal accuracy with high sensitivity [24,25]. In order to accomplish this, it can be combined two emitters or one emitter with dual-emission signal in a system. From here, visual-mode with smartphone-integrated can be designed based on the color tonality.

Nowadays, visual-based detection with naked eye or with the aid of smartphone is attracting great attention for point of care testing (POCT) in healthcare, food production, and environmental monitoring areas [26–31] for detection of ions [32,33], drugs [34,35], biomarkers [36,37], and biomolecules [38]. Naked-eyes have low resolution to distinguish decreasing of color intensity (hue), while they have more strength to differentiate color tonality [39,40]. However, smartphone can also be combined with color tonality system to obtain high detection resolution of the images which can be further processed using many software [41].

For intent of color tonality, many combinations of materials have been used, including metal nanocluster [42], quantum dots [43], carbon dots [27,32], metallic nanoparticles [32] and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [44]. The latter is gained more attention recently. MOFs are porous crystalline materials prepared by metal ions/clusters and organic ligands linking by coordination bonds [45–49]. During the last two decades, MOFs have been intensively investigated for biochemical applications due to their unique physicochemical properties [50–54] including porosity, high surface area, presence of cavities, high thermal stability, and functionality [55–59].

One of the common MOFs that has been used in various applications is MOF-5, which is usually prepared via 1:2 ratio of ligand:metal (terephthalic/Zn2+) [60,61]. However, due to its non-luminescent property, lone MOF-5 has not been used for direct luminescence-based applications. Although many functionalization of MOFs have been reported to prepare luminescent MOFs (LMOFs), such as using fluorescent ligand in MOF-5 [62], insertion of nanoclusters [63], carbon dot [64], and fluorescent dyes [65]. It is more rational and application favorable to prepare MOF-5 with intrinsic luminescence.

Herein, preparation of fluorescent MOF-5 (FMOF-5) is reported using 1:2 metal to ligand ratio of zinc and terephthalic acid (TPA). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on blue emissive fluorescent MOF-5 without functionalization and/or encapsulation of luminescent materials. Interestingly, the blue emission FMOF-5 was tuned towards green-yellow emission with dramatic enchantment after addition of TC via aggregation induced emission (AIE). Thus, a visual-mode ratiometry-based fluorescence was designed for detection of TC that can be applied in pharmaceutical formulations, and residues in serum. Fig. 1 shows a scheme for preparation of FMOF-5 with detection of TC.

All materials and solvents were of analytical grades and used without purification. Zn(CH3COO)2⋅2H2O (99%),Terephthalic acid (TPA, 99.4%), tetracycline (TC, 98%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Thiamine hydrochloride or vitamin B1 (VB1), NaOH, methanol (MeOH), glucose (Glu), lactose (Lac), fructose (Fru), sucrose (Suc), histidine (His), tyrosine (Tyr), tryptophan (Trp), lysine (Lys), glycine (Gly), asparagine (Asp), glutamic acid (GluA), phenylalanine (PheA), serine (Ser), uric acid (UA), NaCl, KCl, Al (NO3)3, CoCl2, Cr(NO3)3, MgSO4, CdCl2, MnCl2, BaCl2, Cu(NO3)2·6H2O were all obtained from Alfa Aeser, Germany. Deionized water was used for the preparation of the solutions. Ultrapure water was prepared with a Millipore system (18.2 MΩ cm).

The following instruments were used for characterization and analysis: transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (TEC9G20, FEI, USA) at 200 KV, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) (MIRA III, TESCAN, Czech), powder X-ray diffraction (PW1730, Netherland), fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) (IRAFFinity-1, Shimadzu), fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary 60, Agilent, USA).

FMOF-5 was synthesized by one step solvothermal method. Briefly, Zn(CH3COO)2⋅2H2O (0.5 mmol), TPA (1 mmol) and NaOH (0.2 mmol) were dissolved in a 50% aqueous methanol solution (20 mL). The reaction mixture was stirred for 15 min and then poured into a reactor, heated for 20 h at 140 °C. The FMOF obtained was blue powder under UV lamp and washed with methanol (50%) for several times and dried at 60 °C in oven. The conventional MOF-5 was prepared following a previously reported method [66]. In brief, zinc acetate dihydrate (40.61 mmol) was dissolved in 7.5 mL of DMF under magnetic stirring (solution A), and TPA(4.10 mmol) was dissolved in 7.5 mL of DMF (solution B). The solution A was added to the solution B under continuous stirring. Then, the resulting solution was poured into a reactor and heated for 24 h at 100 °C. The obtained powder underwent several washing with DMF before being dried in an oven for 24 h.

The fluorescent experiments were performed at room temperature at 350 nm as excitation wavelength. 10 mg of FMOF-5 was dispersed in 10 mL ultra-pure water with stirring for 10 min to obtain a well suspension followed by adding various concentrations of TC solution with continuous mixing. Then, after 30 s the fluorescence spectra of the mixture solutions were scanned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted visual assay for detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: Visual detection experiments were performed at room temperature using UV-lamp (365 nm). 40 mg of FMOF-5 was dispersed in 10 mL ultrapure water with stirring for 10 min, followed by adding TC solutions and mixed thoroughly. After 30 s, the digital photographs were taken (using smartphone) for the mixture solutions. The RGB of the images were analyzed using Image J software or using pre-installed Android-based software of Color Grab App.

The synthesized FMOF-5 showed a strong blue emission under 365 nm irradiation as illustrated in Fig. 2 . Many parameters have a big influence on the synthesis of FMOF-5. One of these is the pH of the solution, as shown in Fig. 2A, the optimum range of pH was betweeen 5–7, out of this range the produced MOF has very weak emission. Different coordination modes in MOFs can be obtained by organic linkers at different pH ranges. In other words, the degree of deprotonation of organic linker increases on increasing the pH value which results in different coordination modes, colors, and fluorescence [67,68]. In addition, the ratio of ligand to metal to obtain high fluorescence of FMOF-5 was 2:1 (L:M) as depicted in Fig. 2B. Although 3:1 gave very close result, but we retained to 2:1 for economic point of view. Futhermore, the optimum solvothermal time to produre high fluorescence of MOF-5 was 20 h as shown in Fig. 2C.

Various characterization techniques were carried out to investigate size, morphology, and optoelectronics properties of FMOF-5. The morphology and structure of the FMOF-5 were characterized by the TEM imaging (Fig. 2D). As it is obvious, the FMOF-5 could have a regular uniform size with size distribution of about 5 nm. The inset histogram as shown in Fig. 2D clearly indicates for relatively monodisperse particles with ±3 nm.

The XRD pattern of FMOF-5 (Fig. 2E) showed consistent peak position and characteristic peaks with the simulated result of MOF-5 [61,67], that suggested a successful synthesis of FMOF-5 by a one-step synthesis strategy confirming its crystalline nature.

FT-IR spectra of the free TPA ligand and FMOF-5 were recorded as displayed in Fig. 2F. The absorption peaks at 1681 cm−1 and 1288 cm−1 are attributed to the CO and O–H vibrations in the carboxyl group of free TPA, respectively [68]. Relatively, the CO absorption band of the ligand coordinated to Zn2+ in FMOF-5 shifts to 1576 cm−1. The FTIR bands between 2644 and 2661 cm−1 disappeared in the FMOF-5 as well as those assigned to OH of carboxylic groups [68,69]. The elemental analysis demonstrated that Zn, C and O elements were homogeneously distributed in the whole structure (Fig. 1S, ESI) [38,39].

The chemical structures of FMOF-5 were investigated by XPS (Fig. 2G, H, I, J). The survey spectrum as shown in Fig. 2G indicates that the elements of Zn, C, and O are existing in FMOF-5. As shown in Fig. 2H, the Zn 2p deconvoluted XPS spectra had two characteristic peaks at 1045.1 and 1021.9 eV, which could be attributed to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, respectively [70,71]. The resolved XPS spectra of O 1s at 532.1 and 531.5 eV (Fig. 2I) were attributed to the CO and O–CO bonds in the FMOF-5 [70]. The XPS signals of C 1s at 284.7, 286.1, and 288.6 eV (Fig. 2J) were attributed to the CC, CO, and O–CO bonds in the FMOF-5 [70,72].

The initial FMOF-5 exhibited a strong blue emission peak at 440 nm when excited at 350 nm (Fig. 3 A) in contrast to the conventional MOF-5 and TPA which had no fluorescence emission as confirmed in control experiments (Fig. 3B). Thus, providing a potential prospect for FMOF-5 to be directly used in fluorescence sensing detection even without functionalization and/or modification with a fluorophore. In the control experiment, neither TPA nor conventional MOF-5 produce any fluorescence emission (Fig. 3B, green and pink line, respectively). Additionally, heating TPA hydrothermally under the same conditions gave very weak emission under 350 nm irradiation. These finding confirms that the strong blue emission is due to the formation of the fluorescent FMOF-5.

Various fluorescence properties of MOFs can be achieved by the careful choice of metal nodes and the rational design of appropriate organic ligands [73,74]. Zinc ion with d10 electronic configuration is a good candidate for the production of fluorescent MOFs because it has various coordination numbers and geometries, and shows intense blue emission when bound to organic ligands [73,75,76]. Moreover, related to the ligand, TPA has a very weak fluorescence (negligible blue emission). While binding of TPA in the FMOF (based on our ligand-metal ratio) might increase the rigidity of the TPA in the FMOF crystal structure. The more rigid the MOF, the less the energy loss due to photo-excitation as heat, which enhance radiation relaxation.

The response of FMOF-5 to TC was investigated (Fig. 3B). After adding a certain concentration of TC, the fluorescence peak of FMOF-5 at 440 nm was almost unchanged while a new peak at 520 nm appeared resulting from tuning of the fluorescent color from strong blue to yellow-green. In contrast, the lone TC solution showed a very weak emission peak at 520 nm. In conclusion, the FMOF-5 can highly enhance the fluorescence of TC virtually ten times or even more. Thus, it can serve as the fluorescent probe for the quantitative analysis of TC based on the ratiometric peak intensity of F520/F440. More interestingly, the dried mixture of FMOF-5 and TC could illuminate with bright yellow-green fluorescence in the solid state.

To investigate details of chemical enhancement mechanism, several investigations were conducted. UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured for TC, FMOF-5 and FMOF-5:TC (Fig. 3C). It is obvious that the absorption spectrum of FMOF-5:TC is a combination of absorption spectrum of TC alone and FMOF-5 alone. This data disproves the development of a new complex between FMOF-5 and TC. Thus, the bright and red-shift fluorescence could be attributed to the aggregation-induced emission caused by TC adsorption on porous FMOF-5 (Fig. 3A). It should be noted that TC was tested with the same concentrations of TPA ligand and zinc ions. The results showed that under the assay conditions, the fluorescence could not be tuned on. These observations strongly suggest that the aggregation-induced TC emission can only respond to FMOF-5 [77].

To further confirm the FMOF-5 response mechanism to TC, several characterization analyses and experimental investigations were conducted namely XRD, XPS, and Zeta potential of FMOF-5 before and after combining with TC. It's interesting to note that the original distinctive peaks of FMOF-5 were still noticeable in the XRD pattern (Fig. 2E), indicating that neither the frame structure nor the crystal structure of FMOF-5 was altered by the addition of TC.

XPS spectra and analysis were used to investigate the mechanism of the aggregation. Carbon and oxygen contents of the FMOF-5 samples were increased after addition of TC as shown by XPS quantification reports (Table S1, ESI), which further suggests that TC entered the framework of FMOF-5. There is no discernible change in the spectra of O 1s, C 1s and Zn 2p of FMOF-5 after the addition of TC (Fig. 2S, ESI), indicating that no chemical reaction occurs between FMOF-5 and TC. Furthermore, Zeta potential was examined to investigate the interaction between FMOF-5 and TC, (Fig. 3S, ESI). The Zeta potential of FMOF-5 was −22.2 mV, whereas the mixture of FMOF-5 and TC was −19.13 mV, indicating that FMOF-5 and TC tended to form aggregates via electrostatic interactions, π-π interactions and hydrogen bonding, because of the π-conjugated six-membered ring structure and the abundance of N, O and H atoms [78].

In order to demonstrate that TC might enter the internal framework of FMOF-5 and emit fluorescence simultaneously, the mixed solutions of FMOF-5 and TC were centrifuged and dried, which were compared with FMOF-5 powder under UV lamp. It was observed that the FMOF-5 powder showed strong blue fluorescence, while the mixed sample, FMOF-5:TC, showed significant yellow-green fluorescence emission. These phenomena validate the hypothesis that TC molecules enter the internal framework of FMOF-5 (Fig. 4S, ESI). These findings support the hypothesis that TC aggregate in the FMOF-5 stiff and fluoresce in a yellow-green color, in other words, FMOF-5 caused the aggregation-induced emission of TC. Therefore, the mechanism of the ratiometric fluorescent probe of FMOF-5 for the detection of TC is thereby thought to be the AIE effect after TC entering the FMOF-5 skeleton.

In order to further verify that the TC responded only to the synthesized material, not to the residual reactants, the reaction of TC with the same concentration of TPA (the ligand) and zinc ions was examined. The results showed that the fluorescence could not be tuned on under the assay conditions. These phenomena surely suggest that the aggregation-induced emission of TC can only respond to FMOF-5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay for determination of TC
ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometry-based fluorimetric assay was conducted as shown in Fig. 4 A. Adding a certain concentration of TC under an excitation wavelength of 350 nm after reacting for 30 s, it can be seen that the fluorescence peak of FMOF-5 at 440 nm was decreased slightly while a new fluorescence peak at 520 nm appeared, resulting change of the fluorescent color from strong blue to strong yellow-green. In contrast, the TC solution without FMOF-5, it showed only a very weak fluorescence emission peak at 510 nm. This phenomenon might indicate that the FMOF-5 can greatly enhance the fluorescence of TC. The slight decrease of the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm might be due to inner filter effect, as there is overlap between excitation spectrum of the FMOF-5 and absorption spectrum of TC.

Moreover, in the ratiometric mode, the blue fluorescence peak of FMOF-5 was used as a self-calibration (reference signal). The ratio of F520/F440 emission intensities was proportional to TC concentration. A dynamic linear detection range of FMOF-5 towards TC was measured from 0 to 70 μM (Fig. 4B) with 5 nM as LOD (when S/N = 3).

To further elucidate the selectivity of the FMOF-5 sensing system, The fluorescence intensity in response to other materials was tested under identical experimental conditions that included common ions (Zn2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Na+, Cr3+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Co2+, NO3 −, Cl−, and SO4 2−), amino acids (Tyr, His, Phe, PheA, Lys, Met, Ser, Trp, GluA, Asp, Cys, Gly, Pro), saccharides (Glu, Fru, Suc), and biomolecules (VB1, UA and AA). As shown in Fig. 4C and D, there was no noticeable change in the emission intensity of the FMOF-5 in the presence of 0.001 M of tested materials. As a result, the FMOF-5 was used as a selective probe for ratiometric detection of TC.

Due to the color tonality of FMOF-5 after the addition of TC, smartphone-based visual detection was employed for the quantitative detection of TC. As the TC concentration increased, the visual probe's color changed from strong blue to strong yellow-green fluorescence emission, which can be seen with the naked eye under UV lamp irradiation (365 nm), as shown in Fig. 5 A.

Image-J software (even pre-installed Android-based Color Grab App) was used to measure the RGB value of the digital photographs of the solutions taken with a smartphone. The visual probe's dynamic linear detection range towards TC was measured from 0 to 30 μM, with LOD as low as 10 nM calculated (when S/N = 3) (Fig. 5B). The multicolor visual ratiometry showed a good multicolor variation from blue to yellow-green as shown in Fig. 5A. In addition, the selectivity of visual sensor toward other metal ions and anions was investigated as shown in Fig. 5C and D. Color change was further confirmed by chromaticity diagram known as Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE). As shown in Fig. 5E, CIE chromaticity was also in accordance with the color change. The visible color change is commonly used for visual detection with the naked eye. As a conclusion, in comparison to previous reports for TC detection, as shown in Table 1 , our presented assay has some advantages, including low-cost, excellent selectivity and sensitivity, a wide linear range of detection, very low LOD, simple preparation, and rapid operation. Based on these advantages, it is highly recommended for point-of-care testing in remote areas or developing countries.

The fluorescent FMOF-5 probe exhibited some unique properties that make it useful in practice. First, in terms of the pH effect, the fluorescence intensity changed slightly across a wide pH range, from 1 to 12, indicating that the FMOF-5 could be used in both acidic and alkaline media (Fig. 6 A). Additionally, the response time is another important factor that must be considered for the tests. The ratiometric intensity (F520/F440) of FMOF-5 was significantly increased immediately after the addition of TC, as shown in Fig. 6B, and remained at equilibrium for a long time afterwards. According to the data the method could meet the requirements of rapid quantitative analysis for real samples. Moreover, the recovery of the original signal or reusability of the MOF were investigated. Another advantage is resuability, which is an important feature of the sensors that demonstrates robustness and cost-effectiveness. The fluorescence intensity of the FMOF-5 has been nearly stable for more than ten times (Fig. 6C), indicating that the fluorescent sensor is a highly reusabe. Furthermore, shelf-life, stability under ambient conditions, and the fluorescence sensor in water were investigated because the stability of FMOF-5 in solvent is an important property of MOFs. As shown in Fig. 6D, the fluorescence of the FMOF-5 is almost stable after being stored for about three months at 4 °C and room temperature. Based on the aforemnetioned figures of merits, we conclude that the FMOF-5-based probe is very useful for practical appplications and point-of-use settings.

In order to further verify the feasibility of the developed ratiometric fluorescent strategy for detecting TC in human serum, the samples were performed on real samples spiked with TC at different concentrations (2, 4, 8 μM), and the recoveries for TC were calculated (Table 2 ). The results also show that the developed ratiometric fluorescent strategy can be used to detect TC in biological and pharmaceutical systems, offering a novel approach for rapid and effective monitoring of TC in complex samples like real biological fluids.

A novel ultra-small zinc-based MOF sensing system, FMOF-5, has been successfully developed for ratiometric smartphone-assisted visual detection of TC. Nanodots of FMOF-5 as small as 5 nm were prepared using facile solvothermal method. When TC is present, the fluorescence color of the sensing system changes from blue to yellow-green, which can be detected by integrating a smartphone. The sensing method exploited the strong blue emission of FMOF-5 with the yellow-green fluorescence caused by the AIE effect of TCs. The visual sensor can be manufactured at a low cost by reducing the need for time-consuming, sophisticated sample pretreatment, high-cost precursors, and TC detection instruments. More importantly, the smartphone-based detection with FMOF-5 makes it an excellent candidate for quantitative point-of-care and real-time testing with high accuracy not only in health safety monitoring but also in bioanalysis.

Sameera Sh. Mohammed Ameen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing Original Draft.Nidhal M. Sher Mohammed: Conceptualization, Review & Editing, Supervision, Project Administration, Khalid M. Omer: Conceptualization, Review & Editing, Supervision.
